section.title: Manasseh and Amon
section.sectionRationale: Manasseh reverses every reform of his father Hezekiah, rebuilding high places, worshiping the host of heaven, sacrificing his own son, practicing sorcery, and filling Jerusalem with innocent blood, provoking the LORD's decisive verdict that Judah will be wiped out like Samaria. His son Amon continues the same evil briefly before being assassinated, setting the stage for Josiah's reforms.
section.thesis: Manasseh's fifty-five year reign of unprecedented idolatry and bloodshed becomes the specific, named reason the LORD gives for Judah's coming destruction, showing that even generations of later repentance under Josiah cannot ultimately reverse a judgment this decisively provoked.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Manasseh's unprecedented idolatry and violence, verses 1 to 9; the LORD's decisive verdict against Judah, verses 10 to 18; and Amon's brief, equally evil reign and assassination, verses 19 to 26.

units.label: Manasseh's unprecedented idolatry
units.note: he did great evil in the sight of the LORD

verses.text: Manasseh was twelve years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem fifty-five years. His mother's name was Hephzibah.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD by following the {{toevah|abominations}} of the nations that the LORD had driven out before the Israelites.

For he rebuilt the high places that his father Hezekiah had destroyed, and he raised up altars for Baal. He made an Asherah pole, as King Ahab of Israel had done, and he worshiped and served all the host of heaven.
Manasseh also built altars in the house of the LORD, of which the LORD had said, "In Jerusalem I will put My Name."
In both courtyards of the house of the LORD, he built altars to all the host of heaven.
He sacrificed his own son in the fire, practiced {{qesem|sorcery}} and divination, and consulted mediums and spiritists. He did great evil in the sight of the LORD, provoking Him to anger.

Manasseh even took the carved Asherah pole he had made and set it up in the temple, of which the LORD had said to David and his son Solomon, "In this temple and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, I will establish My Name forever.

I will never again cause the feet of the Israelites to wander from the land that I gave to their fathers, if only they are careful to do all I have commanded them, the whole Law that My servant Moses commanded them."
But the people did not listen and Manasseh led them astray, so that they did greater evil than the nations that the LORD had destroyed before the Israelites.
And the LORD spoke through His servants the prophets, saying,
"Since Manasseh king of Judah has committed all these abominations, acting more wickedly than the Amorites who preceded him, and with his idols has caused Judah to sin,
this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Behold, I am bringing such calamity upon Jerusalem and Judah that the news will reverberate in the ears of all who hear it.

I will stretch out over Jerusalem the measuring line used against Samaria and the plumb line used against the house of Ahab, and I will wipe out Jerusalem as one wipes out a bowl, wiping it and turning it upside down.
So I will forsake the remnant of My inheritance and deliver them into the hands of their enemies. And they will become plunder and spoil to all their enemies,
because they have done evil in My sight and have provoked Me to anger from the day their fathers came out of Egypt until this day.'"

Moreover, Manasseh {{shaphak|shed}} so much innocent {{dam|blood}} that he filled Jerusalem from end to end, in addition to the sin that he had caused Judah to commit, doing evil in the sight of the LORD.
As for the rest of the acts of Manasseh, along with all his accomplishments and the sin that he committed, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And Manasseh rested with his fathers and was buried in his palace garden, the garden of Uzza. And his son Amon reigned in his place.

units.label: Amon's brief, evil reign
units.note: the servants of Amon conspired against him

verses.text: Amon was twenty-two years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem two years. His mother's name was Meshullemeth daughter of Haruz; she was from Jotbah.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, as his father Manasseh had done.
He walked in all the ways of his father, and he served and worshiped the idols his father had served.
He abandoned the LORD, the God of his fathers, and did not walk in the way of the LORD.
Then the servants of Amon conspired against him and killed the king in his palace.
But the people of the land killed all those who had conspired against King Amon, and they made his son Josiah king in his place.
As for the rest of the acts of Amon, along with his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And he was buried in his tomb in the garden of Uzza, and his son Josiah reigned in his place.

toevah.gloss: Abomination, something detestable to the LORD, a term used throughout the Law for practices utterly incompatible with covenant worship. That Manasseh personally followed the toevah of the nations Israel had displaced marks his reign as a direct reversal of the very conquest that established Israel in the land.

qesem.gloss: Divination or sorcery, explicitly forbidden in Deuteronomy 18:10-12 as an attempt to access hidden knowledge or power apart from the LORD. Manasseh's practice of qesem alongside consulting mediums represents a wholesale embrace of exactly the occult practices Israel's law had specifically warned against.

dam.gloss: Blood, here specifically innocent dam that Manasseh shed so extensively it filled Jerusalem from end to end. This bloodshed, likely including persecution of faithful prophets and worshipers of the LORD who resisted his idolatry, is named alongside his idolatry as a decisive reason for the coming judgment.

shaphak.gloss: To pour out or shed, used here of Manasseh pouring out innocent blood throughout Jerusalem. The same verb describes both violent bloodshed and, elsewhere, the pouring out of God's own wrath, underscoring the direct, cause-and-effect relationship between Manasseh's shed blood and the calamity the LORD now pours out in response.

basic.context: Manasseh, Hezekiah's son, reigns an extraordinary fifty-five years, the longest of any king of Judah, and uses that time to systematically reverse his father's reforms, rebuilding high places, erecting altars to Baal and the host of heaven even within the LORD's own temple, sacrificing his own son, practicing sorcery and divination, and shedding so much innocent blood that it filled Jerusalem. The LORD responds through His prophets with a decisive, specific verdict, comparing Judah's coming judgment directly to Samaria's fall and Ahab's house, and naming both Manasseh's idolatry and his bloodshed as the cause. His son Amon continues the same evil for a brief two-year reign before being assassinated by his own servants, and the people install his son Josiah as the next king.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific king's uniquely severe unfaithfulness and the LORD's specific, prophetic response; it is not a general statement that any particular sin automatically guarantees national judgment regardless of later repentance, though it does show how severe and sustained covenant violation can set consequences in motion that even later reform cannot fully reverse. What endures is the sober truth that persistent idolatry and violence against the innocent provoke real, named divine judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: Manasseh personally embraces the toevah, abominations, of the very nations Israel had displaced, practices qesem, sorcery, explicitly forbidden by the Law, and sheds so much innocent dam, blood, that it fills Jerusalem, an act the text describes with shaphak, poured out, the same verb elsewhere describing God's own outpoured wrath, now foreshadowing the calamity about to be poured out on Judah in response.
basic.typology: Manasseh's decisive, named role in provoking Judah's coming judgment, despite generations of later partial reform under Josiah, anticipates the New Testament's sober teaching that some sins and their consequences work themselves out in history even after forgiveness is later extended, a pattern only fully and finally resolved at the cross, where Christ absorbs the complete weight of judgment for all who trust in Him.
basic.christ: Manasseh's shedding of so much innocent blood that it filled Jerusalem stands in the sharpest possible contrast to Christ, whose own innocent blood was shed once for all, not to provoke judgment but to satisfy it completely on behalf of others, Hebrews 9:12, showing that the very city Manasseh filled with unjust bloodshed would one day witness the shedding of blood that saves rather than condemns.
basic.god: God is patient across an extraordinarily long fifty-five year reign of open, systematic covenant violation, yet His patience is not indifference; when judgment finally comes, it is specific, named, and directly tied to particular sins, idolatry and innocent bloodshed, showing that God's justice, however long deferred, precisely answers real and serious moral failure rather than acting arbitrarily.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 18:9-12 explicitly forbids the divination and sorcery Manasseh practices in this chapter. 2 Chronicles 33:10-13 records additional detail that Manasseh was later captured by Assyria and, in humiliation, repented and was restored, a genuine but ultimately insufficient personal turning that did not reverse the national judgment already provoked. Jeremiah 15:4 later cites Manasseh by name as the specific reason the LORD would not relent from judging Judah, confirming the lasting weight of this chapter's verdict.
basic.oneStory: Manasseh's unprecedented fifty-five years of idolatry and bloodshed becomes the specific, named reason the LORD gives for Judah's coming destruction, showing that even the genuine, later reforms of Josiah cannot fully undo consequences this severe, a sobering pattern only finally and completely resolved when Christ's own innocent blood, shed once for all, satisfies the judgment that generations of shed innocent blood in Jerusalem had provoked.

context.literary: The chapter's structure moves from an extended, escalating catalogue of Manasseh's specific idolatrous acts, to the LORD's uniquely direct and lengthy prophetic verdict quoting God's own words at length, to a brief coda on Amon's short, equally evil reign, closing the chapter on a note of unresolved crisis just before Josiah's dramatic reforms begin.
context.historical: 2 Chronicles 33:11-13 adds the detail that Manasseh was taken captive to Babylon by Assyrian forces during his reign, an episode independently plausible given Assyrian practices of the era, and records his subsequent humbling and partial restoration, information the author of Kings chooses not to include, focusing instead on the lasting national consequences of his earlier and more extensive unfaithfulness.
context.canonical: This chapter's decisive prophetic verdict against Judah, explicitly citing Manasseh's sins, is directly recalled in Jeremiah 15:4 as the specific reason judgment could not ultimately be averted even after Josiah's reforms, showing this chapter functions as the specific turning point explaining Judah's otherwise surprising fall despite Josiah's subsequent faithfulness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Manasseh's uniquely severe and prolonged covenant violation, including systematic idolatry and widespread bloodshed, as the specific, named cause of the LORD's decisive verdict that Judah would be destroyed like Samaria, a judgment so provoked that even later reform under Josiah could not fully avert it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical king's uniquely severe unfaithfulness and God's specific prophetic response to it; it does not prescribe that any and all national sin will inevitably and irreversibly guarantee identical judgment regardless of later repentance, though it clearly shows that severe and sustained covenant violation can set real consequences in motion that later partial reform cannot fully undo.

debates.question: Why does the author of Kings omit Manasseh's later Assyrian captivity, humbling, and partial restoration recorded in 2 Chronicles 33:11-13?

views.label: A deliberate focus on lasting national consequence rather than personal redemption
views.case: The book of Kings consistently emphasizes the covenantal and national trajectory toward exile as its central theological concern; including Manasseh's late personal repentance, while true and recorded elsewhere, would not change the fact that the national judgment his earlier decades of sin provoked remained fixed, so the author's selective emphasis serves his larger purpose of explaining Judah's coming fall.

views.label: Different authorial purposes and sources between Kings and Chronicles
views.case: Kings and Chronicles are widely recognized as having different theological emphases, with Chronicles often more interested in individual repentance and restoration as encouragement for a post-exilic audience, while Kings, written to explain the exile's cause to a nation already experiencing or anticipating it, naturally emphasizes the accumulated weight of sin leading to judgment rather than a late-life reversal that did not ultimately change the national outcome.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that whatever personal repentance Manasseh may have later experienced, as Chronicles records, the author of Kings intentionally centers this account on the specific, decisive, and lasting national consequences of his earlier decades of sin, which Jeremiah 15:4 later confirms remained the driving cause of Judah's judgment.

study.originalLanguages: Manasseh personally embraces the toevah, abominations, of the displaced nations, practices qesem, sorcery, explicitly outlawed by the Law, and sheds so much innocent dam, blood, that it fills Jerusalem end to end, an act described with shaphak, poured out, the same verb elsewhere used of God's own wrath being poured out, directly foreshadowing the specific calamity the LORD now declares will be poured out upon Judah in response to these very sins.

typology.type: Innocent blood shed throughout Jerusalem, provoking decisive divine judgment
typology.fulfillment: The innocent blood of Christ, shed once for all in the very same city, not to provoke judgment but to satisfy it completely, Hebrews 9:12.

typology.type: Consequences of severe, sustained sin that later partial reform cannot fully reverse
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's sober teaching that while forgiveness is fully available in Christ, some earthly consequences of sin still work themselves out in history, fully and finally resolved only at the cross, Galatians 6:7-8.

study.christ: Manasseh's filling of Jerusalem with innocent blood stands in the starkest possible contrast to Christ, whose own perfectly innocent blood was shed in that same city, not to provoke further judgment but to fully and finally satisfy it on behalf of all who trust in Him, Hebrews 9:12. Where Manasseh's decades of sin set in motion consequences that even Josiah's genuine, later reforms could not fully reverse, Christ's one perfect sacrifice fully and permanently reverses the judgment provoked by every sin, for all who are united to Him by faith, accomplishing what no human reformer, however sincere, could ever achieve.
study.god: God's patience extends across an extraordinary fifty-five year reign of open, systematic covenant violation, yet this patience is never indifference; when His judgment finally comes, it is specific, deliberately named, and directly tied to particular sins, idolatry and innocent bloodshed, demonstrating that His justice, however long deferred, precisely answers real and serious moral failure rather than acting arbitrarily or capriciously.

crossReferences.note: Explicitly forbids the divination and sorcery Manasseh embraces, showing his practices as direct violations of the Law he was bound to uphold.
Records Manasseh's later Assyrian captivity and personal repentance, additional detail the author of Kings chooses not to include in this account.
Later cites Manasseh by name as the specific reason the LORD would not relent from judging Judah, confirming the lasting theological weight of this chapter's verdict.
Christ's own innocent blood, shed once for all, stands in complete contrast to the innocent blood Manasseh shed throughout Jerusalem.

study.oneStory: Manasseh's unprecedented fifty-five years of systematic idolatry and widespread innocent bloodshed becomes the specific, named cause of the LORD's decisive verdict against Judah, a judgment so thoroughly provoked that even Josiah's genuine and extensive later reforms cannot fully avert it. This sobering account finds its ultimate resolution only in Christ, whose own innocent blood, shed once in that same city, fully and finally satisfies the judgment that generations of shed innocent blood had provoked, for all who trust in Him.

quiz.q: How did Manasseh's religious practices compare to his father Hezekiah's reforms?

options.text: He systematically reversed them, rebuilding the high places, erecting altars to Baal and the host of heaven, and even placing idols within the LORD's own temple.
options.note: Verses 3 to 7 record this exact and extensive reversal of Hezekiah's reforms.

options.text: He continued and expanded his father's reforms further.
options.note: The text describes a complete reversal of Hezekiah's reforms, not their continuation or expansion.

options.text: He made no changes to Judah's worship practices at all.
options.note: The text describes extensive, deliberate changes reintroducing idolatry throughout Judah.

options.text: He removed idolatry even further than his father had.
options.note: The text explicitly describes Manasseh reintroducing and expanding idolatry, the opposite of further removal.

quiz.q: What two specific sins does the LORD's prophetic verdict name as provoking Judah's coming judgment?

options.text: Manasseh's abominable idolatry and the innocent blood he shed throughout Jerusalem.
options.note: Verses 11 to 16 name both of these specific sins as the direct cause of the coming calamity.

options.text: Judah's failure to pay proper tribute to Assyria.
options.note: The prophetic verdict is entirely religious and moral in nature, not concerned with tribute payments to Assyria.

options.text: A famine that had struck Judah during this period.
options.note: No famine is mentioned as the cause; the verdict specifically names idolatry and bloodshed.

options.text: No specific reasons are given for the coming judgment.
options.note: Verses 11 to 16 give extensive and specific reasons for the coming judgment.

quiz.q: To what previous judgments does the LORD compare the coming calamity on Jerusalem?

options.text: The measuring line used against Samaria and the plumb line used against the house of Ahab.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly makes this exact comparison to two previous, well-known judgments.

options.text: The judgment on Sodom and Gomorrah.
options.note: The specific comparisons given are to Samaria and the house of Ahab, not to Sodom and Gomorrah.

options.text: No prior judgment is referenced for comparison.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly and specifically references two prior judgments for direct comparison.

options.text: The judgment on Egypt at the Exodus.
options.note: The text specifically names Samaria and Ahab's house, not the Exodus judgment on Egypt.

quiz.q: How did Manasseh's reign end, and who succeeded him?

options.text: He rested with his fathers and was buried in his own palace garden, and his son Amon reigned in his place.
options.note: Verse 18 records this succession, a peaceful death unlike his grandson's violent end.

options.text: He was assassinated by his own servants like his son Amon later would be.
options.note: The text describes a peaceful death and burial for Manasseh; it was his son Amon who was later assassinated.

options.text: He died in battle against a foreign invading army.
options.note: The text simply states he rested with his fathers, indicating a natural death rather than death in battle.

options.text: He had no successor and Judah's monarchy ended with him.
options.note: The text explicitly names his son Amon as his direct successor.

quiz.q: How did Amon's reign conclude, and how did the people of the land respond?

options.text: His own servants conspired against him and killed him in his palace, but the people of the land killed the conspirators and made his son Josiah king instead.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 record both the assassination and the people's decisive response installing Josiah.

options.text: He reigned peacefully for many decades before a natural death.
options.note: The text explicitly states he reigned only two years before being assassinated by his own servants.

options.text: The conspirators against Amon successfully seized the throne themselves.
options.note: The people of the land killed the conspirators and installed Josiah instead, preventing them from seizing power.

options.text: No successor was established after Amon's death.
options.note: The text explicitly names Josiah as the next king installed by the people of the land.

questions: Manasseh's reign lasted fifty-five years, showing that God's patience with persistent sin can extend far longer than we might expect. Where might you be presuming on God's patience regarding an ongoing pattern of sin in your own life?

The LORD named specific sins, idolatry and bloodshed, as the cause of coming judgment rather than treating the calamity as random misfortune. How comfortable are you naming specific sins as the real cause of difficult consequences in your own life, rather than viewing them as unrelated bad luck?

Even Josiah's genuine and extensive later reforms could not fully undo the judgment Manasseh's sins had provoked. Where might you need to recognize that repentance, while always welcomed by God, does not always erase every earthly consequence of past choices?

Amon walked in his father's evil ways rather than learning from the warnings surrounding Manasseh's reign. What example, good or bad, from someone before you are you currently at risk of simply repeating rather than critically examining?

This chapter sits between two contrasting reigns of reform, Hezekiah's before and Josiah's after, showing how quickly gains can be reversed. What spiritual gains in your own life or community need ongoing protection rather than being assumed as permanent?

translationNotes.note: Manasseh's fifty-five year reign is the longest of any king of Judah or Israel recorded in the Bible, making the extent of his sustained unfaithfulness especially significant to the author's argument.
The innocent blood Manasseh shed likely included persecution of faithful prophets and worshipers of the LORD who resisted his idolatry, though the text does not specify every victim by name.

The author of Kings notably omits the account found in 2 Chronicles 33:11-13 of Manasseh's later Assyrian captivity and personal repentance, choosing instead to emphasize the lasting national consequences of his earlier, more extensive unfaithfulness.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 21:1-26. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
